The strongest association observed to date is between increased BLLs in children and reductions in IQ scores. The threshold below which lead is no longer associated with adverse neurodevelopmental effects cannot be identified.
Data in experimental animals corroborate findings in humans and also indicate a risk of cancer upon exposure to inorganic lead. Based on findings in animals, the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) has classified inorganic lead compounds as probably carcinogenic to humans (Group 2A). However, a guideline based on decreased IQ would be more conservative and considered protective for all cancer- and non-cancer-related effects of exposure to lead. Lead in drinking water is not a concern by inhalation or dermal absorption, so a multi-route exposure assessment was not performed.
A MAC of 0.005 mg/L (5 µg/L) is proposed for lead in drinking water, based on the following considerations:
- The MAC must be measurable. The U.S. EPA has established a PQL of 0.005 mg/L, based on the ability of laboratories to measure lead within reasonable limits of precision and accuracy using approved methods. There is no similar process in place to establish a PQL specific to Canada.
- The MAC must be achievable at reasonable cost. Municipal-scale treatment technologies can remove lead from drinking water; however, lead is mostly present in drinking water from leaching in the distribution and plumbing systems. Consequently, strategies for minimizing lead at the tap should focus on controlling corrosion and removing lead-containing components. The use of materials certified to the appropriate NSF/ANSI standards, such as Standard 61 (Drinking Water System Components—Health Effects) and Standard 372 (Drinking Water System Components—Lead Content), will help reduce the concentration of lead at the tap.
- The proposed MAC will have a significant impact on the BLLs of children, the most vulnerable population. It is estimated that reducing the MAC from 0.01 to 0.005 mg/L would lower the geometric mean percentage of children with BLLs exceeding 5 µg/dL by 7.2 percentage points (from 9.4% to 2.2%).
- As the primary source of lead in drinking water is the leaching from plumbing and distribution system components, a private residential drinking water treatment device, certified to the appropriate NSF/ANSI standard, is the best option for reducing lead concentrations in drinking water at the tap. However, the use of such devices should not be considered a permanent solution.
In considering both treatment and analytical achievability and the health risks associated with exposure to lead from drinking water, the Federal-Provincial-Territorial Committee on Drinking Water has proposed a MAC of 0.005 mg/L (5 µg/L) for total lead in drinking water, based on a sample of water taken at the consumer’s tap, using the appropriate protocol for the type of building being sampled. As this value exceeds the drinking water concentration associated with neurodevelopmental effects in children, every effort should be made to maintain lead levels in drinking water as low as reasonably achievable (or ALARA).
As part of its ongoing guideline review process, Health Canada will continue to monitor new research in this area and recommend any change to the guideline that is deemed necessary.

## Appendix B: Provincial and territorial anticipated impacts
Note: The information below has been provided by the individual province or territory and is included in the language in which it was received.

### Prince Edward Island
No paragraph has been provided by the province.

### Newfoundland and Labrador
The Province of Newfoundland and Labrador is responsible for extensive monitoring for inorganic parameters, including lead, in the province.  Lead monitoring is conducted semi-annually for all public water supplies in the province, and quarterly for populations larger than 5,000. pH is also included in the extensive monitoring program conducted in the province.  pH in the province is typically low and below the guideline of 6.5-8.5.  There are typically greater than 300 pH exceedances in the province annually.  As discussed in the technical document, the low pH in the province causes issues with corrosion in distribution systems and affects the lead concentration levels in drinking water. In recent years, there have been increasing numbers of lead exceedances during summer months from service line/water fixtures in buildings at the existing guideline levels.
There could be a high cost of implementing the proposed guideline for lead in Newfoundland and Labrador.  Due to naturally low pH issues experienced by many water systems in the province, prevention of corrosion in distribution systems would require new treatment systems and/or significant upgrades to existing systems.  Many smaller systems would have to consider changing from gas chlorination to hypo-chlorination to avoid decreasing pH levels.  Many systems would also have to install pH adjustment systems or upgrade their existing pH adjustment systems to maintain appropriate pH levels within the distribution system. Water pH in some communities is so low, that even with treatment, it would be difficult to dose enough chemical to meet both pH and lead guidelines.
There are two public groundwater systems that have naturally occurring lead in the province.  One system has a lead removal system which may not have the capability to reduce the levels below the proposed guideline as a number of recent results are above 0.005 mg/L.   The other system has no removal system and has been on a non-consumption advisory since 2002.
The province will continue to monitor for lead and pH at the current frequency, and continue to assist communities in ensuring their pH adjustment systems are working effectively to increase pH levels and keep corrosion control measure in place.
The revised guideline proposes a reduction to the maximum acceptable concentration (MAC) for lead from 10 µg/L to 5 µg/L. The proposed reduction is likely to result in moderate to significant impacts for Nova Scotia.
Available data suggests that utilities will not be impacted as a result of the proposed reduction; however, this data represents flushed samples collected from the distribution system and is not representative of lead concentrations at the consumer's tap. Existing data prevents an accurate determination of the true impact. The known presence of lead service lines, solders, and fittings in utility  distribution systems and plumbing of older homes coupled with the lack of enforceable corrosion control requirements likely result in consumer exposures to lead levels  that are higher than what is reported.
For Nova Scotia, the revised guideline will require the development  and enforcement of  lead sampling protocols ‎for residential/non-residential sites. This will likely result in challenges for the utilities and the Compliance Division of Nova Scotia Environment. Specific challenges are likely to include:  lack of willing participants; access for sample collection; costs associated with lead service line replacement ; and increased costs for laboratory analysis, etc.
New Brunswick is supportive of minimizing exposure to lead from drinking water. At this time we are unable to quantify the impact of the proposed guideline, but anticipate that it will likely be substantial.
Additional discussion is required with stakeholders to discuss how to proceed with implementation of the proposed guideline and how to manage compliance, monitoring and enforcement. Mitigative efforts, and the associated costs, could be substantial.  It is reasonable to assume that systems owners will resist feeling responsible for water quality within buildings (which will vary from building to building) particularly those that have many internal plumbing issues. New Brunswick has many systems with aggressive water - particularly those that use surface water. Corrosion control will need to be part of this discussion.  Replacement of lead service lines and some internal plumbing will be required. The cost associated with these measures could be immense and will be different for every system depending on a variety of factors. This will have to be reviewed on a case-by-case basis.
Based on drinking water data that we currently have, changing the current MAC from 10 µg/L to 5 µg/L will result in the percentage of regulated municipal water systems with a historical exceedance of the guideline to increase from 15% (10 of 67 systems) with at least one result ≥10 µg/L to 22% (15 systems) with at least one result ≥5 µg/L; and crown systems with at least one historical exceedance of the guideline will increase from 13% (27 of 202 systems) to 17% (35 systems).
However, the true impact will very likely be much greater. New Brunswick’s historical monitoring data for lead has been obtained based on flushed samples as is recommended in the current guideline.  It is impossible to know at this time the lead concentrations that will be found at individual taps once sampling is completed at recommended locations and in accordance the newly introduced sampling protocols of the proposed guideline, but it is reasonable to assume that lead results will increase.
For private wells, historical water quality data suggest that the changed MAC will result in an increase in exceedance from 3% to 7%. This could result in increased costs associated with analytical testing and treatment.
Au Québec, de janvier 2013 à décembre 2014, 23 158 analyses du plomb ont été réalisées dans le cadre du Règlement sur la qualité de l’eau potable, dans un total de 3022 réseaux de distribution. Tous les réseaux assujettis au Règlement doivent réaliser annuellement le contrôle du plomb dans l’eau qu’ils distribuent et le nombre d’échantillons à prélever par année est en fonction du nombre de personnes desservies.
Sur la période de janvier 2013 à décembre 2014, 393 (1,7 %) des analyses réalisées ont présenté un résultat supérieur à la norme actuelle de 10 µg/L, ce qui a touché 85 réseaux de distribution (2,8 %). La concentration maximale mesurée a atteint 977 μg/L, tandis que la médiane des résultats dépassant la norme était de 16 µg/L. À titre comparatif, 845 résultats d’analyse de plomb (3,6 %) auraient dépassé la recommandation canadienne proposée de 5 µg/L durant cette période, pour un total de 177 réseaux touchés (5,9 %).
Considérant les résultats d'analyse disponibles, les impacts attendus d'une éventuelle réduction de la norme de plomb dans la réglementation québécoise en fonction de la révision de la recommandation proposée seraient importants. En effet, cela pourrait faire doubler le nombre de réseaux de distribution devant mettre en place des mesures correctrices, celles-ci pouvant varier selon la taille du réseau et l’ampleur du problème observé.
A reduction in the Canadian Drinking Water Quality Guideline for lead proposed in this document will have an impact in Ontario as the province typically adopts federal guidelines as enforceable standards.
In the case of lead, the main source is associated with water delivery, particularly the isolated components (i.e., pipes and fittings) within buildings and homes. The drinking water system owners/operators have little control over these components as their obligation is to deliver water that is not corrosive.
Ontario recognizes that the impact of lead can be mitigated by appropriate flushing practices and replacement of components such as lead service lines that contribute to lead levels in the drinking water over time.
A review conducted by the Manitoba Office of Drinking Water in 2012 found that some of the largest public water systems in the province have lead service lines in their distribution systems. This includes many of the largest urban centres and some old towns. In some of those systems, total lead levels remained above the current (i.e., 10 µg/L) lead guideline even after flushing the taps for 5 minutes. The water was left to sit in service lines for at least 6 hours but no more than 24 hours before sampling. The overall number of lead service lines in public water distribution systems across the province is estimated at 30,000 to 50,000. Immediate full replacement is not an option as it would be prohibitively expensive. However, many of the concerned large public water systems have established lead service line replacement programs in their respective communities, providing incentives for homeowners to replace the portion of lead service line going from the curb stop into individual properties. This process is slow and many homeowners find the financial contribution required from them to be too high. Annual lead service line replacement costs are expected to remain significant on a province-wide basis for the next several years. In addition, based on the monitoring strategy recommended by Health Canada, monitoring for total lead at the consumer’s tap in residential dwellings, as well as monitoring at drinking water fountains and cold water taps in schools, multi-dwelling residences, and large buildings would cost several hundred thousand dollars annually. Based on the foregoing, we anticipate the impact of revisions to the lead guideline - including lowering the guideline for lead in drinking water to 5 µg/L and proposed monitoring recommendations - to be significant in Manitoba.
The Water Security Agency (WSA) has reviewed the proposed guideline document for Lead in Drinking Water and agrees with the proposed MAC of 5 microgram/L for total lead in drinking water.  Drinking water quality data for 2,760 samples collected in the last 5 years (2010 to present) from the distribution systems of municipalities regulated by the WSA showed that total lead levels exceeded the proposed MAC only in few locations in the province.  Some of the municipalities in Saskatchewan continue to replace lead service lines in the distribution system, and others have some form of replacement programs including providing an incentive to home owners to replace their lead service lines from the curb stop to the building entry.  The Water Security Agency follows the corrosion control guidance document of Health Canada in dealing with lead exceedences in the distribution system and encourages the waterworks owner to replace the lead service lines where related exceedences occur.  Since studies showed that many of the water treatment systems, including conventional water treatment filtration systems and RO systems, are effective in removing lead, and since these type of treatment systems are in existence and/or are being increasingly adopted for use in WTPs in the province, the WSA believes and expects that the new standard for total lead will have minimal or no impacts including cost to municipal and other regulated waterworks in the province.  Further, since lead is present mostly in drinking water due to many factors including age of the pipeline/components and from leaching in the distribution and plumbing systems, the WSA continues to work with municipalities and the Ministry of Health/Health Regions in Saskatchewan in monitoring lead levels in the distribution systems.
No impact paragraph has been provided by the province.
Given the developmental risks to children from lead, BC supports minimizing lead intake from drinking water as much as reasonably achievable, preferably below the existing and the proposed maximum in the Guidelines for Canadian Drinking Water Quality.
The proposed change in the guideline will cause the water delivered by a small number of water supply systems to exceed the new, more stringent MAC. However, leaching from fixtures within individual buildings is likely the most significant contributor to lead concentration in water, and therefore exceedance of the guideline at the tap. Drinking water in many water systems in BC is soft (low in hardness), and slightly acidic (low pH and alkalinity). When soft water stagnates in building piping, lead and other metals can leach out from the plumbing into the water. This is particularly true in older facilities and homes that may have lead and copper or brass plumbing, fixtures, fittings, or used solder containing lead.
It is therefore difficult for water suppliers to know lead concentrations delivered at individual taps, as this will vary from building to building, depending on plumbing. Sampling for buildings, particularly high risk institutions such as schools and daycares, will remain a cost pressure. Questions of whether this is responsibility of water suppliers or building owners will also still remain.
Water suppliers do not have direct control over water once it leaves their distribution system, however in areas where water is aggressive, effective mitigation measures can be taken at either the individual or the community level. Individual building owners may reduce lead concentration in water by regular flushing of taps, retrofitting plumbing, installing corrosion control, or installing other point of use treatment. At a community level, water suppliers may adopt corrosion control measures to reduce the corrosiveness of water before it enters the distribution system. None of these measures are without significant expense, and the most cost effective of these options would likely depend on the size of the water supply system.
It is not possible to quantify any potential impact of the guideline technical document for lead in Yukon for large public drinking water systems (LPDWS) with piped distribution systems due to a lack of exposure data. Currently, LPDWS are required to routinely monitor treated water for chemical parameters including lead prior to entry into the distribution system.  All systems produce water below the proposed MAC. There is no requirement for routine monitoring of chemical parameters in the distribution system.
